ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers fried potato products as the closest parent; no single-entry exact code for generic restaurant-style french fries.

serving.natural_language_portion: One regular serving of french fries is approximately 117 g (a medium fast-food portion), providing around 365 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, as purchased (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 11400)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for frozen french fries (NDB 11400 / FDC ID 170697), which represents the closest standardised match for generic deep-fried potato strips as served in a restaurant context. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical salted restaurant preparation (~250 mg/100 g) based on USDA Branded Food composite data.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Potato, french fries (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 (glucose reference = 100) for french fries is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Am J Clin Nutr 87(1):214S–234S. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 41.0 × 1.17) / 100 ≈ 32.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~250 mg/100 g) and high energy density
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit french fry intake due to moderate sodium content (~250 mg/100 g) and the tendency to consume large portions; choosing unsalted or lightly salted preparations and restricting portion size to ≤100 g can help manage sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 75) and high carbohydrate content (41 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: French fries carry a high glycemic index (GI 75) and a high glycemic load per serving (~32), causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; people with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption and pair with high-fibre or protein-rich foods if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (15 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.3 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of deep-fried french fries is associated with increased cardiovascular risk due to elevated total fat, saturated fat, and residual trans fat from frying oils; individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise intake and prefer baked or air-fried potato alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: French fries (炸薯条) are strips of potato that have been deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft inside. They are one of the most widely consumed fast-food and snack items globally, served at fast-food chains, hawker stalls, cafes, and restaurants including in Singapore. The dish is typically seasoned with salt immediately after frying, and may be accompanied by ketchup, chilli sauce, mayonnaise, or other condiments. Per 100 g, french fries provide approximately 312 kcal, 3.4 g protein, 15 g fat, and 41 g carbohydrate, with 3.5 g dietary fibre and 250 mg sodium. The high glycemic index (GI 75) and high glycemic load make them a concern for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The fat content, including saturated and trace trans fats from frying, raises cardiovascular risk with frequent consumption. Sodium content is moderate but can be higher in heavily salted commercial preparations. Potassium (579 mg/100 g) and vitamin B6 (0.27 mg/100 g) are notable micronutrients inherited from the potato base. French fries are naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and are suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets when fried in plant-based oil. In Singapore, they are commonly sold as a side dish or snack at fast-food outlets, kopitiam stalls, and casual dining restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories are in french fries?
What is the GI of french fries?
How much fat is in french fries?
What are the macros in french fries?
Is 炸薯条 high in carbs?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for frozen french fries, which is the closest standardised approved-source match. Sodium has been adjusted to reflect typical salted restaurant preparation. GI value of 75 is directly supported by the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for plain french fries was identified; hence confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not consistently reported for this food in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for this food. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — french fries are a plant-derived food containing no vitamin B12 (naturally zero). Cholesterol (cholesterol_mg) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — this product is fried in vegetable oil with no animal-derived ingredients (naturally zero). The food_name in the input JSON is '炸薯条' (Chinese for french fries); the canonical English name 'French Fries' has been assigned. Cuisine tag 'Chinese' in the input reflects the restaurant context (Keming Bing Sat, a Chinese kopitiam); however, french fries are an internationally ubiquitous food of Belgian/American origin and have been classified accordingly. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules given the Singapore GrabFood source context. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data for fried potato products.

improvement_actions_taken: Assigned canonical English food_name 'French Fries' from Chinese input '炸薯条'.
Applied Singaporean multilingual naming rules (English, Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given Singapore GrabFood source context.
Sourced nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170697) as closest approved-source match; sodium adjusted for typical salted restaurant preparation.
GI 75 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 AJCN.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg) with explicit notes.
Documented confirmed-zero values (vitamin_b12_mcg, cholesterol_mg) with 'naturally zero' / 'confirmed zero' justification phrases in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Ontology codes verified: FoodOn FOODON_00002694, FoodEx2 A0EJX (closest_parent), SNOMED CT 226803007 (exact). All match_type values use only allowed strings.
Health context includes Type 2 Diabetes with machine key type2_diabetes and human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
